name: Sage

book_claims.claim: One of the richest sources of potent antioxidants with potential cognitive-enhancing and protective effects
book_claims.mechanism: Contains an impressive range of biologically active compounds. Medieval School of Medicine considered it essential for health.
book_claims.study_ref: Ref 108 in book (animal studies + preliminary human trials)

supplementary_research.finding: Double-blind RCT found Salvia officinalis extract significantly improved cognitive function in patients with mild-to-moderate Alzheimer's disease over 4 months compared to placebo. Supports historical and book claims about cognitive enhancement.
Sage extract improved memory and attention in healthy young adults in a dose-dependent manner. Both immediate and delayed word recall improved significantly versus placebo.

Systematic review of 8 human trials found sage (Salvia species) consistently improved cognitive performance and mood. Mechanisms include acetylcholinesterase inhibition, antioxidant activity, and anti-inflammatory effects.

Notable evidence for cognitive enhancement in both healthy adults and Alzheimer's patients. Sage contains compounds that inhibit acetylcholinesterase, similar to the mechanism of pharmaceutical Alzheimer's drugs like donepezil.

culinary_pairings.source: Sage butter (burro e salvia) is the classic sauce for ravioli and gnocchi
Essential in Tuscan bean soups (ribollita, pasta e fagioli)
Classic autumn pairing in risotto, ravioli fillings, and roasted dishes
Both Lamiaceae herbs; combined in Italian roasts and herb blends
Foundational aromatic combination in Mediterranean cooking
Sage honey is a prized single-varietal honey in Mediterranean regions

seasonality: Perennial herb available year-round in temperate climates. Grows easily in pots near windows. Fresh sage has more nuanced flavour than dried, though dried sage is more concentrated. Best harvested before flowering.

nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Extraordinarily high polyphenol content -- among the highest of any herb. Includes rosmarinic acid, carnosic acid, and diverse flavonoids.
Water-soluble; well-extracted in sage tea. Anti-inflammatory and antioxidant.
Bioactive monoterpene with acetylcholinesterase inhibitory activity. Safe in culinary amounts; excessive concentrated essential oil intake should be avoided.
Triterpenoid with anti-inflammatory and anticancer activity. Low oral bioavailability improved by fat co-consumption.

synergies.description: Both Lamiaceae herbs with overlapping but complementary antioxidant profiles
Book recommends alternating sage infusions with green tea for varied polyphenol intake
